Tomato___Bacterial_spot.symptoms: Small dark water-soaked spots on leaves and fruit
treatment: Remove and destroy all infected plant parts immediately
Apply copper-based bactericide spray every 7 days
Avoid working with plants when they are wet
Disinfect all tools with 10% bleach solution after use
Improve air circulation by pruning overcrowded stems
prevention: Rotate crops — do not plant tomatoes in same spot for 2 years
Use drip irrigation instead of overhead watering

Tomato___Early_blight.symptoms: Dark brown spots with yellow rings on older leaves
treatment: Remove infected lower leaves and dispose away from farm
Apply copper-based fungicide or mancozeb every 7–10 days
Water at the base — never from above
Stake plants to improve airflow
Apply organic mulch to prevent soil splash onto leaves

Tomato___Late_blight.symptoms: Large dark greasy patches on leaves; white mould on undersides
treatment: Act immediately — this disease destroys crops within days
Remove and burn ALL infected plant material
Apply chlorothalonil or mancozeb fungicide immediately
Spray the entire field including healthy-looking plants
Repeat spraying every 5–7 days until controlled
prevention: Monitor crops daily during rainy or humid weather

Tomato___Leaf_Mold.symptoms: Yellow patches on upper leaf surface; olive mould on undersides
treatment: Remove heavily infected leaves immediately
Apply fungicide containing chlorothalonil or copper
Reduce humidity by increasing plant spacing
Improve ventilation especially in greenhouse settings
Avoid wetting leaves when watering

Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.symptoms: Many small circular spots with dark borders and grey centres
treatment: Remove infected leaves starting from the bottom of the plant
Apply mancozeb or chlorothalonil fungicide every 7–10 days
Mulch around the base to prevent soil splash
Stake plants to improve airflow
Avoid overhead watering
prevention: Rotate tomatoes with non-related crops for 3 years
Remove all plant debris after harvest

Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.symptoms: Tiny yellow speckling on leaves; fine webbing on undersides
treatment: Spray plants forcefully with water to knock off mites
Apply neem oil spray (2 tbsp neem oil per litre of water)
Use insecticidal soap spray on affected areas
Introduce natural predators like ladybugs if available
Apply miticide if infestation is severe
prevention: Keep plants well-watered — mites thrive on stressed plants
Avoid dusty conditions around the farm
Monitor the undersides of leaves weekly

Tomato___Target_Spot.symptoms: Brown spots with concentric rings on leaves, stems, and fruit
treatment: Remove and destroy infected leaves
Apply azoxystrobin or chlorothalonil fungicide
Improve air circulation by pruning
Avoid wetting the foliage when irrigating
prevention: Keep field free of weeds and plant debris

Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.symptoms: Leaves curl upward and turn yellow; stunted plant growth
treatment: Remove and destroy infected plants immediately
Control whiteflies using yellow sticky traps
Apply imidacloprid insecticide to kill whitefly vectors
Use reflective mulch to repel whiteflies
No chemical cure — focus on removing infected plants

Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.cause: Viral infection spread by contact and infected tools
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.symptoms: Mottled light and dark green patches on leaves; distorted growth
treatment: Remove and destroy all infected plants
Disinfect hands and tools with soap or bleach after touching plants
Control aphids and insects that spread the virus
Do not smoke near tomato plants — tobacco carries this virus
Your tomato plant looks healthy — keep it up!
Continue regular watering at the base of the plant
Apply balanced fertilizer (NPK) every 2–3 weeks
Monitor weekly for early signs of disease or pests

Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.symptoms: Small powdery brick-red pustules scattered on both leaf surfaces
treatment: Apply fungicide containing azoxystrobin or propiconazole
Spray at first sign of pustules — early treatment is key
Remove and destroy heavily infected leaves
Repeat fungicide application every 14 days if needed
prevention: Plant early in the season to avoid peak rust conditions

Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.symptoms: Long cigar-shaped grey-green lesions (5–15 cm) along the leaf
treatment: Apply azoxystrobin, propiconazole or mancozeb at tasseling stage
Remove severely infected leaves to slow spread
Increase plant spacing to improve air circulation
Avoid late-day irrigation — wet nights favour this fungus
prevention: Rotate maize with soybeans or other non-host crops
Till or bury crop residue after harvest to destroy spores

Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.symptoms: Rectangular pale brown to grey lesions running parallel to leaf veins
treatment: Apply strobilurin-based fungicide (azoxystrobin) at first sign
Remove extra leaves if plants are overcrowded
Avoid overhead irrigation
Spray whole field including surrounding border rows
prevention: Plant gray leaf spot resistant varieties
Till soil to bury infected residue after harvest

treatment: Your maize plant looks healthy — great condition!
Continue regular watering especially during tasseling and grain fill
Top-dress with nitrogen fertilizer at knee-high stage
Scout weekly for early signs of pests or disease
prevention: Keep the field free of weeds
Monitor for fall armyworm during vegetative stages
Rotate crops each season to break disease cycles
